data.title: Columbia Banking System signals accelerated Pacific Premier integration and 15% operating return on equity as tech investments deepen
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Columbia Banking System, Inc. (COLB) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Clint E. Stein opened with, "Our second quarter operating results were up 14% from the year ago quarter. Our improved performance is a product of our focus on profitability, balance sheet optimization, and the impact of our operational efficiency initiative we executed during the first half of 2024." Stein highlighted ongoing net interest margin expansion, increased core fee income, and disciplined expense management. He stated, "Collaboration across teams...enabled us to win business and attract new relationships," emphasizing a strategic focus on profitability and credit quality over pure growth.
  • Stein announced, "Columbia is positioned to not only navigate the current environment but to capitalize on strategic opportunities, including our upcoming acquisition of Pacific Premier. Integration planning remains on track...we received overwhelming approval for the transaction." He added, "What Pac Premier brings to the tech stack is so impressive that we recently announced internally that the Pac Premier Chief Information Officer, will remain as the CIO of Columbia."
  • Stein detailed a shift in investment strategy: "Considering the market density Pac Premier provides us, we are shifting this investment to the Intermountain states, specifically Utah and Colorado as we look to build a meaningful presence organically in these markets."
  • He also noted a key management change: "We expanded our executive leadership talent with the addition of Judi Giem, who joined Columbia in June as our CHRO."
  • CFO Ronald L. Farnsworth reported, "We reported second quarter EPS of $0.73 and operating EPS of $0.76. Operating return on average tangible equity was 16.85%, while operating PPNR increased 14% from the first quarter to $242 million."
  • Farnsworth added, "Our NIM increased 15 basis points to 3.75% this quarter."
  • President of Consumer Banking, Christopher M. Merrywell, commented that recent deposit campaigns brought in over $450 million in new core deposits and generated new SBA relationships.

Outlook

  • Stein reiterated, "We remain laser-focused on optimizing our financial performance and enhancing long-term tangible book value. We also expect to return excess capital to our shareholders." He stated, "We expect our acquisition of Pacific Premier to meaningfully enhance our capital generation capabilities, which already exceed what is required to support prudent growth and our regular dividend."
  • Stein projected, "As we integrate Pacific Premier, we will have additional flexibility to return excess capital."

Financial Results

  • Farnsworth stated, "Operating noninterest income was $65.1 million for Q2 was up $8 million or 14%, reflecting strong core fee income growth."
  • The provision for credit loss was $29 million for the quarter, and "our overall allowance for credit losses remained robust at 1.17% of total loans."
  • Tangible book value per share increased by 3%, and regulatory capital ratios continued to build, with Tier 1 common at 10.8% and total risk-based capital ratio at 13%.
  • Operating expenses were reported at $269 million, with annual lists and compensation offset by lower services, marketing and other expense, along with lower intangible amortization.
  • Merrywell noted, "Loan growth was centered in commercial portfolios during the quarter. As owner-occupied CRE and commercial line balance increases offset multifamily and residential loan contraction."

Q&A

  • David Pipkin Feaster, Raymond James: Asked about the drivers of double-digit increase in loan originations and client sentiment. Stein responded, "It's a combination...we're not really experiencing what we'd call leakage through the back door with customers going elsewhere," and Nixon added, "Production for the quarter...roughly 30% higher than Q1 and about 18% higher than Q2 last year."
  • Feaster also questioned the Pacific Premier acquisition timeline and balance sheet optimization. Stein answered, "We want to take advantage of getting the day 1 fair value marks...we have 0 credit concerns."
  • Feaster inquired about initiatives to increase fee income. Nixon explained, "We have a predictive analytics program...got like a 50% closure rate on that," and cited year-over-year growth in treasury management, commercial card, merchant services, international banking, and trust.
  • Matthew Timothy Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked about securities growth and borrowings related to the Pacific Premier deal. Farnsworth stated, "We added $600 million of par...goal of that was to reduce the pro forma asset sensitivity with the combined company post close."
  • Clark asked about deposit growth outlook and pricing. Merrywell responded, "We've slowed the repricing...we see some competitors that will theoretically go out there and offer up in excess of 4% for certain things...we're feeling pretty good about our ability to compete."
  • Clark further asked about cleaning up the capital stack. Stein replied, "We've done some analysis and we look at what the capital stack of our peer banks looks like...we expect that to accelerate with Pac Premier."
  • Jeffrey Allen Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about maturity schedule for transactional assets. Stein replied, "If the Fed were to lower rates probably what we're on 150 basis points or so...otherwise...it really starts to pick up from a repricing standpoint, '27, '28."
  • Rulis asked about margin trajectory. Farnsworth said, "Month of June margin would have been 3.79% adjusted for your timing differences throughout the quarter."
  • Rulis inquired about expense run rate. Stein said, "If you go with the $270 million and cash at forward, it's going to be a little bit light because it's not going to include that level of investment."
  • Christopher Edward McGratty, KBW: Asked about pro forma earning asset base. Farnsworth confirmed, "At close, we'll net sell $0.5 billion of PPBI bonds...pay down the wholesale funding."
  • McGratty questioned if the combined margins of Columbia and Pacific Premier have structurally changed. Farnsworth said, "Our margins increased compared to 90 days ago."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was constructive but probing, especially regarding loan growth, acquisition timing, margin drivers, and expense outlook.
  • Management maintained a confident and direct tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, with Stein stating, "We remain laser-focused on optimizing our financial performance and enhancing long-term tangible book value."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence increased, particularly around the Pacific Premier integration and capital position, while analysts maintained a consistent level of scrutiny.
  • Stein’s repeated focus on disciplined growth and capital return, as well as Farnsworth’s detailed responses, reflected steady confidence in the company’s strategic direction and financial health.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language in Q2 2025 emphasized acceleration of Pacific Premier integration and a shift in geographic investment focus to the Intermountain states, whereas Q1 2025 focused on the Southern California market and initial merger announcement.
  • Key metrics improved, with operating EPS rising from $0.67 in Q1 to $0.76 in Q2, and operating PPNR increasing from $212 million to $242 million.
  • NIM expanded by 15 basis points in Q2, after contracting modestly in Q1.
  • Management’s tone in Q2 was more optimistic, reflecting progress on integration and greater flexibility for capital return, compared to a more cautious but positive outlook in Q1.
  • Analyst focus shifted from merger rationale and regulatory hurdles in Q1 to operational execution, fee income initiatives, and margin sustainability in Q2.

Risks and Concerns

  • Stein acknowledged, "Macroeconomic uncertainty around tariffs is causing companies to pivot in a manner best suited for their business."
  • Deposit balances declined due to seasonal activities and customer use of cash for investments or debt repayment, serving as a headwind for growth.
  • Management highlighted minimal operational impact from Pacific Premier integration, with only 2% of staff focused on integration activities.
  • Repricing and runoff in transactional loan portfolios remain an earnings headwind, but management emphasized prioritizing profitability and credit quality.

Final Takeaway

Columbia Banking System’s second quarter results demonstrated improved profitability and operating leverage, supported by higher net interest margin, core fee income gains, and disciplined expense management. The company’s focus on integrating Pacific Premier, advancing its technology stack, and strategically expanding into the Intermountain states signals confidence in future growth and capital return prospects. Management emphasized a commitment to optimizing performance and enhancing shareholder value while navigating current macroeconomic challenges and maintaining strong credit quality.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Scholastic targets 20% adjusted EBITDA growth in fiscal 2026 amid cost savings and IP expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Scholastic Corporation (SCHL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Peter Warwick, President and CEO, stated that Scholastic "delivered strong financial and strategic results in the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025" with adjusted EBITDA growth in line with original guidance and effective cost controls that positioned the company for earnings growth in fiscal 2026. He highlighted that revenue growth was driven by the performance of the Children's Book Publishing and Distribution segment and the acquisition of 9 Story Media Group. Warwick noted, "In the U.S. alone, the book [Sunrise on the Reaping] sold over 2 million copies in its first month."
  • Warwick emphasized continued capital return to shareholders, with over $35 million in dividends and share repurchases in the fourth quarter and a total of $92 million for the fiscal year. He also discussed significant organizational changes, stating, "We began executing on a set of significant organizational changes, work that's continued into quarter 1 that we believe will strengthen leadership, enhance growth and improve efficiencies."
  • Warwick announced the integration of Trade Publishing, Book Fairs, and Book Clubs into a new Children's Book group under Sasha Quinton's leadership, with Jackie De Leo joining to further strengthen the team. He described this as a move to "unlock further efficiencies and potential in our vertically integrated children's book publishing and distribution."
  • Haji Glover, CFO, reported, "Revenue increased 7% to $508.3 million in the fourth quarter and was up 2% to $1,625.5 million for the fiscal year." He added, "Adjusted operating income decreased to $63.4 million in the fourth quarter from $66.8 million in the prior year period." Glover noted that lower adjusted operating income was primarily due to amortization expenses from the 9 Story acquisition. He also stated, "Adjusted EBITDA increased 1% to $91.2 million in the fourth quarter and was up 6% to $145.4 million for the fiscal year."

Outlook

  • Scholastic is targeting adjusted EBITDA of $160 million to $170 million for fiscal 2026, with Warwick stating, "At the midpoint of guidance, adjusted EBITDA is expected to grow 20%, excluding $10 million in incremental tariff expense currently anticipated from implemented or announced tariffs."
  • Fiscal 2026 revenue is expected to grow 2% to 4%. Glover explained that this reflects "strength in our core businesses, partially offset by continuing headwinds on consumer spending."
  • The company expects free cash flow in fiscal 2026 of $30 million to $40 million, attributing this to "higher expected earnings, improved working capital and lower cash tax, partially offset by higher capital investments and other accrued expenses."
  • Glover detailed that cost-saving actions taken are expected to result in $10 million of additional benefit in fiscal 2026, with incremental $15 million to $20 million in further cost savings planned.

Financial Results

  • In the Children's Book Publishing and Distribution segment, fourth quarter revenue increased 9% to $288.2 million. Trade Publishing revenue rose 19% to $97.3 million, driven by the new Hunger Games title. Book Fairs revenue increased 5% to $177.8 million, while Book Clubs revenue was $13.1 million in the quarter.
  • Education segment revenue was $125.7 million, down 7% from the prior year period.
  • Entertainment segment revenue was $14.8 million compared to $0.6 million in the prior year period, reflecting the 9 Story Media Group acquisition.
  • International segment revenue increased 8% to $76.8 million.
  • Free cash flow was $29.2 million for fiscal 2025, and net debt at year-end was $136.6 million, primarily reflecting the 9 Story acquisition and capital returned to shareholders.
  • The company returned over $92 million to shareholders in fiscal 2025, including nearly 3.5 million shares repurchased.

Q&A

  • Brendan Michael McCarthy, Sidoti & Company, LLC: Asked about sources of cost savings for fiscal '26. Haji Glover: "A majority of the cost actions are coming out of non -- more discretionary functions, things that we can cut back on as we're looking to be more frugal as an organization."
  • McCarthy: Asked if overhead would decrease in fiscal '26. Glover: "We're getting the full year impact of the stuff right in FY '26. So the FY '25 stuff as we showed in -- we talked about in the script, we're experiencing -- we saw $15 million of that in FY '25, and we're going to see another $10 million in FY '26."
  • McCarthy: Questioned expectations for flat revenue in Education. Warwick: "The market continues to be cyclically difficult, especially with districts and schools but we do expect the situation to progressively improve just because of the normal cyclicality of the education business."
  • McCarthy: Asked about the pipeline for state-sponsored programs. Warwick: "I'm pretty optimistic that things are going well, both in terms of being able to expand what we do in those states... we have multiple conversations which are going on with state governments."
  • McCarthy: Inquired about internal feedback from combining trade and school channels. Warwick: "The main impact at the moment is really internal rather than external. And it's been tremendous, actually... we have the opportunity to be able to link what we're selling in schools through the book fairs and book club channels in a much more integrated way than we've done before with the Trade Publishing."
  • McCarthy: Asked about entertainment profitability. Glover: "We expect it to be slightly lower, but in line with this year."
  • McCarthy: Requested timing for real estate monetization and capital allocation priorities. Glover: "We're hoping, as I mentioned earlier, to have something within the next 90 to 120 days. In terms of our capital allocation priorities, we're going to remain consistent with returning capital to shareholders as possible when we can."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions centered on cost savings, education segment outlook, and real estate monetization, with a neutral tone and requests for clarity rather than pressing skepticism.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing successful cost management, growth initiatives, and optimism about future performance. Warwick concluded, "I'm excited about the new fiscal year ahead. I mean, I think we're in good momentum. We've got a positive outlook, and we continue to focus on creating shareholder value and impact, that's so important to us."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analyst and management sentiment remained steady, with management slightly more confident due to recent organizational changes and strong year-end financials.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for adjusted EBITDA increased from a previous forecast of approximately $140 million for fiscal 2025 to a new range of $160 million to $170 million for fiscal 2026, targeting 20% growth at the midpoint.
  • Revenue growth expectations remain modest, with continued pressure on education and consumer segments. Strategic focus has shifted toward integrating business units and implementing cost-saving programs.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from concerns about macroeconomic headwinds and funding uncertainty in education to more detailed questions on cost actions and capital allocation.
  • Management's confidence increased as they highlighted tangible cost reductions, integration benefits, and forward progress on capital structure optimization.
  • The company advanced its real estate monetization strategy, moving from disclosure of potential asset values to active engagement with Newmark Group for sale-leaseback transactions.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing headwinds in consumer and school spending, with the education segment experiencing revenue decline due to a pressured supplemental curriculum market.
  • Incremental tariff expenses of $10 million are anticipated for fiscal 2026, primarily impacting nonbook and novelty items.
  • Entertainment segment profitability faces inflationary pressures and production timing uncertainties, with most new production benefits expected in fiscal 2027.
  • The company is repositioning the education business for sustainable growth, but short-term revenue is expected to remain flat.
  • Analysts noted the long sales cycles and uncertainties in state-sponsored programs.

Final Takeaway

Scholastic enters fiscal 2026 with reinforced confidence, targeting robust adjusted EBITDA growth of 20% at the midpoint and implementing significant cost-saving measures. Strategic integration across book publishing and distribution, capital returns to shareholders, and real estate monetization initiatives are expected to support further value creation, while the company remains focused on navigating persistent market pressures and unlocking the potential of its iconic IP portfolio.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SkyWest anticipates 14% block hour growth and $10 EPS for 2025 amid fleet expansion and tariff management
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SkyWest, Inc. (SKYW) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Russell A. Childs highlighted, "Today, SkyWest reported net income of $120 million or $2.91 per diluted share for the second quarter of 2025. These results reflect the second quarter's higher production and the ongoing exceptionally strong demand for our products." Childs also announced, "Last month, we announced an agreement to purchase and operate 16 new E175s under a multiyear contract with Delta with deliveries expected to begin in 2027. We also secured firm delivery positions with Embraer for 44 more E175s from 2028 to 2032."
  • Childs stated, "If implemented, tariffs on Brazil are not expected to change our 2025 production forecast. However, we are not willing to pay a 50% tariff on new aircraft deliveries."
  • CFO Robert J. Simmons said, "Today, we reported a second quarter GAAP net income of $120 million or $2.91 earnings per share. Q2 pretax income was $163 million. Our weighted average share count for Q2 was 41.4 million, and our effective tax rate was 26%." Simmons also explained, "We now expect our 2025 GAAP EPS could be in the $10 per share area if we are successful in executing on the opportunities in front of us."
  • Chief Commercial Officer Wade J. Steel detailed, "During the quarter, we announced a new flying agreement with Delta for 16 new E175s under a multiyear flying contract. The 16 new E175s are expected to replace 11 SkyWest-owned CRJ900s and 5 CRJ700s that we are currently operating."

Outlook

  • Management now anticipates 2025 block hours to be up approximately 14% over 2024, an increase from the previous quarter's 12% to 13% guidance. Simmons indicated, "The improved outlook in our 2025 block hours is driven primarily by improving fleet utilization and availability and ongoing strong demand for our production."
  • Simmons provided, "We now expect our 2025 GAAP EPS could be in the $10 per share area if we are successful in executing on the opportunities in front of us. We continue to expect to deliver solid operating leverage with 14% year-over-year production growth translating into a roughly 28% increase in EPS in 2025."
  • The company expects to operate 30 CRJ550s by the end of 2025 and deploy 14 new E175s for United and Alaska by end of 2026, with 16 new E175s for Delta in 2027 and 2028.
  • Simmons noted that capital expenditures for 2025 are expected to be approximately $575 million to $625 million.

Financial Results

  • Total Q2 revenue of $1 billion is up from $948 million in Q1 2025 and $867 million in Q2 2024. Contract revenue reached $842 million, prorate and charter revenue was $145 million, and leasing and other revenue was $47 million.
  • The quarter included $23 million of previously deferred revenue recognized, with a cumulative $286 million of deferred revenue expected for future periods.
  • Cash ended at $727 million, with debt at $2.5 billion, and the company generated $68 million in free cash flow during Q2.
  • SkyWest bought back 195,000 shares for $17.3 million and invested $169 million in CapEx, including the purchase of 2 new E175s and 4 additional CRJ900s.

Q&A

  • Hillary Cacanando, Deutsche Bank: Asked about opportunities for the CRJ200 fleet. Steel responded, "The CRJ200 fleet is a fleet that gives us a lot of flexibility... Our current priority levels would be to continue to fly those and add more to current contracts with our major partners."
  • Cacanando also inquired about MRO challenges and parting out 29 airframes. Steel explained, "There are challenges at the moment, especially on the CRJ side with parts and labor... It is improving every day. MHI is working diligently."
  • Savanthi Nipunika Prelis-Syth, Raymond James: Queried about tariffs and cost implications. Childs explained, "The tariff that we have paid so far at the 10% is not on the full aircraft... there's a lot of American components in the 175, particularly the engine."
  • Prelis-Syth asked for updates on the SkyWest Charter authorization. Childs responded, "We're very optimistic about that... We're very excited and confident that Bryan Bedford will be an exceptional FAA administrator."
  • Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen: Questioned the recovery in the prorate and small community market. Childs stated, "Small, midsized communities have very, very strong demand for our product... The foundational economics of service in small communities is stronger than it ever has been."
  • Fitzgerald also asked about capital allocation if CapEx is deferred. Simmons said, "We've got a lot of options. We've obviously proven ourselves capable and willing to buy back our own stock."
  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: Inquired about 2026 block hour growth. Steel replied, "We do feel optimistic there are still opportunities out there for us."
  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: Asked about capital allocation amid higher CapEx. Simmons said, "We feel very good about the optionality that we've got as a result of our strong capital position and liquidity."
  • O'Brien also clarified if delivery timing optimization was demand-driven. Childs responded, "The demand is not our problem for sure."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts frequently pushed on supply chain constraints, tariffs, and capital allocation, with a slightly positive to neutral tone, expressing optimism about demand but concern about operational challenges.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone throughout, especially when discussing demand and flexibility. Childs stated, "We remain optimistic about 2025 and 2026. We continue to play the long game and invest in our fleet and future to ensure we are in the best possible situation to respond to market demand."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts demonstrated increased confidence, particularly as guidance was raised and operational leverage materialized.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Block hour growth guidance increased from 12%-13% to approximately 14%, and EPS outlook was raised from the low to mid-$9 range to potentially $10 per share for 2025.
  • Strategic focus has shifted to securing additional fleet flexibility with new Embraer orders and Delta agreements, as well as managing tariff risks.
  • Analysts remained focused on supply chain and tariff uncertainties, while management's tone became more confident regarding demand and financial resilience.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, free cash flow, and share repurchases increased quarter-over-quarter, supported by higher production.
  • Management's confidence in fleet flexibility and ability to manage macroeconomic risks has grown, reflected in their language and capital allocation strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing supply chain and labor challenges in the third-party MRO network, particularly affecting the CRJ fleet.
  • Tariff uncertainty on Brazilian aircraft imports remains a significant risk, with management unwilling to accept a 50% tariff and indicating willingness to delay deliveries if necessary.
  • Steel noted, "We do think it will continue on, and that's why we have purchased these assets. So we don't know exactly how long, but what we did want to do is derisk the situation."
  • Childs emphasized strong relationships with partners and manufacturers to navigate these risks, stating, "We are not willing to pay a 50% tariff on new aircraft deliveries."

Final Takeaway

SkyWest delivered robust Q2 results, highlighted by surging demand, increased guidance for block hours and EPS, and strategic fleet expansion agreements with major partners. Management emphasized their preparedness to navigate ongoing macroeconomic risks, including tariffs and supply chain constraints, while maintaining operational flexibility and a strong capital position to drive future growth and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil rebounds on U.S.-E.U. trade optimism, U.S. crude stock draw
data.description:
Crude oil turned higher for the first time in five sessions, as progress in tariff negotiations between the Trump administration and the European Union eased market concerns over the global economic outlook.

The U.S. and E.U. reportedly are working toward a deal that would set a 15% tariff for most E.U. goods to the U.S., which would follow a similar U.S. deal with Japan, and the prospect of a successful trade deal has sparked a risk-on tone and boosted investor sentiment.

"The main theme overnight was risk-on," Commerzbank analysts Tommy Wu and Moses Lim wrote. "The latest trade developments spurred optimism among investors that the U.S. could reach more trade deals with its trading partners before the impending deadline on August 1."

Meanwhile, the U.S. posted a larger than expected 3.2M-barrel decline in crude stocks and a 1.7M-barrel draw in gasoline last week, indicating summertime demand in the world's largest crude consumer remains relatively strong.

Oil price gains were interrupted briefly by reports that the Trump administration plans to allow Chevron to again pump oil in Venezuela; the U.S. imported 170K bbl/day of Venezuelan oil in April before Chevron's license expired, down from nearly 300K bbl/day in December and January, according to EIA data.

Oil rebounded late in the session on news that Russia plans to cut gasoline exports to all but a few allies and countries with which it has supply agreements.

Front-month Nymex crude for (CL1:COM) September delivery closed +1.2% to $66.03/bbl, and front-month September Brent crude (CO1:COM) ended +1% to $69.18/bbl, the biggest gain for both benchmarks in a week.

U.S. natural gas futures edged higher after the EIA reported a below-normal inventory build of 23 Bcf for last week, leaving inventories at 3,075 Bcf or 171 Bcf above the five-year average; the front-month Nymex August contract settled +0.5% to $3.094/MMBtu.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (NYSEARCA:XLE)

data.title: Old Second Bancorp signals optimism for 2025 with Evergreen Bank integration and margin durability
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old Second Bancorp, Inc. (OSBC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • James L. Eccher, President, CEO & Chairman, opened by reporting net income of $21.8 million or $0.48 per diluted share for the second quarter. He noted, "Return on assets was 1.53%. Second quarter 2025 return on average tangible common equity was 15.29% and the tax equivalent efficiency ratio was 54.54%." Eccher highlighted that earnings were impacted by a $531,000 MSR mark-to-market loss and an $810,000 merger-related expense due to the Bancorp Financial merger, but asserted, "profitability Old Second remains exceptionally strong and book value continued to compound heading into the July 1 close of the Evergreen Bank acquisition." He pointed to a tangible equity ratio increase to 10.83% and common equity Tier 1 at 13.77%. Eccher emphasized, "Our focus now is on the effective integration of Evergreen Bank and optimizing the balance sheet for its impacts."
  • Bradley S. Adams, COO & CFO, stated, "Net interest income increased by $1.3 million or 2.1% to $64 million for the quarter ended June 30 relative to the prior quarter of $62.9 million, also increased $4.5 million or almost 8% from the year ago like quarter." He added, "Old Second should continue to build capital as evidenced by the 144 basis point improvement in the TCE ratio over the past year, which means we have added $1.78 of tangible book value over the last 12 months, pretty strong performance." Adams remarked on the company's share repurchase, saying, "we repurchased approximately 327,000 shares and a privately negotiated transaction at a modest discount to market." He concluded, "I'm very bullish that our market will remain at exceptionally strong levels over the remainder of the year and into next."

Outlook

  • Management expressed confidence in their positioning post-Evergreen integration, with Eccher stating, "We remain confident in our positioning and extremely exciting with what the Evergreen Bank transaction will add for us." Adams added, "you also mentioned bias slightly higher. I think that from a rate standpoint, things still feel roughly balanced to me in terms of status quo versus some level of recession and/or rate cuts."
  • Adams provided color on expense outlook: "Overall, we are hopeful we can keep core expense growth, exclusive of acquisitions in the kind of 4% area."
  • In response to analyst questioning, Adams suggested the margin outlook is "flat plus or minus 10 basis points instead of plus or minus 5," due to the complexities of the Evergreen fair value marks.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income rose to $64 million, up $1.3 million from the previous quarter.
  • The tax equivalent net interest margin was 4.85% for the quarter, a 3 basis point decrease from 4.88% last quarter.
  • Average deposits increased $51 million quarter-over-quarter.
  • The allowance for credit losses on loans increased to $43 million as of June 30, or 1.08% of total loans, from $41.6 million or 1.05% last quarter.
  • Noninterest expense for the quarter was $1.1 million less than the prior quarter.
  • Book value and capital ratios improved quarter-over-quarter, with tangible equity ratio up 49 basis points and common equity Tier 1 at 13.77%.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Allen Rulis, D.A. Davidson: Asked about Evergreen conversion timing and expense run rate. Adams replied, "The conversion, we expect to be kind of early fourth quarter type range, early to mid...by the time we report fourth quarter and kind of December is the first time that you'll see a closer to final run rate on operating expenses and then first quarter should be relatively clean."
  • David Joseph Long, Raymond James: Questioned commercial client sentiment and loan growth. Eccher responded, "Appetite for CapEx has been muted...We are seeing pockets of growth in our leasing group and some in commercial real estate...second quarter for us, that was our strongest growth quarter in over 2 years."
  • Nathan James Race, Piper Sandler: Probed charge-off outlook post-Evergreen. Eccher explained, "in powersports lending in general, loss rates can run a little bit higher...losses in that book could be anywhere between what, 1% and 1.5%."
  • Brian Joseph Martin, Janney: Asked about margin durability in a flat rate environment. Adams stated, "our margin is stable and durable -- slightly higher with what Evergreen brings to the table."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was probing and focused on integration, expense management, and future margin sustainability, with a generally neutral to slightly positive sentiment as management provided detailed responses.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and responses, using phrases such as "feel exceptionally good about how we're positioned" and "very bullish that our market will remain at exceptionally strong levels."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased optimism, especially regarding Evergreen integration and margin outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize the optimistic bias for post-acquisition performance, with management now more bullish on margin durability and capital strength.
  • Strategic focus shifted from balance sheet remediation and credit improvement in Q1 to integration, expense discipline, and leveraging Evergreen's asset mix in Q2.
  • Key metrics, including net income, tangible equity ratio, and net interest income, all improved quarter-over-quarter.
  • Analyst focus in Q2 centered more on integration details, margin outlook, and cost synergies compared to Q1's emphasis on credit issues and nonperforming assets.
  • Management's confidence increased, with more forward-looking positive statements about capital and margin.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged the potential volatility from interest rate movements and the complexity of integrating Evergreen, but asserted that "provision levels quarter-over-linked quarter reflect no material change as our projections continue to be aligned with the prior quarter's assumptions."
  • Adams acknowledged "more margin movement for us right now just in balance sheet movements" and indicated that some uncertainties remain due to ongoing fair value work.
  • Analyst questions highlighted possible higher loss rates from Evergreen's lending portfolio and integration risk, to which management responded by referencing strong collateral positions and proactive loss reserving.

Final Takeaway

Old Second Bancorp's management maintains a confident outlook for 2025, highlighting robust capital positions, improved profitability, and successful initial integration of Evergreen Bank. The team signals optimism for further growth and margin durability, while acknowledging upcoming expense noise and integration complexities in the near term. Expectations for loan growth and margin performance remain positive as the bank leverages new business lines and focuses on disciplined expense management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: McGrath RentCorp raises 2025 revenue outlook to $960M as modular business expands
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joseph F. Hanna stated the company "delivered solid second quarter results. Rental operations grew by 5% and adjusted EBITDA grew by 3%." He indicated the company's core strategy remains focused on being a modular solutions provider, with the Mobile Modular division showing an 8% increase in total revenues. Hanna highlighted that "quote activity was healthy, and our June rental revenue backlog was up year-over-year," and noted strong commercial wins around larger infrastructure projects as well as continued solid funding for the education business.

  • Hanna shared that modular sales revenues increased 13%, and initiatives like Mobile Modular Plus and site-related services delivered "healthy increases in the quarter, helping to offset lower units on rent." He also noted that Enviroplex, the classroom manufacturing business in California, "had a good quarter. Sales revenues and margins were strong for the quarter."

  • Portable Storage rental revenues decreased 5% year-over-year but improved 5% sequentially. Hanna described recent shipment trends as encouraging and stated, "Close ratios and rental rates are holding steady, and we are maintaining our discipline on winning new orders." TRS-RenTelco rental revenues grew by 7%, with utilization ending the quarter at 65%. The rental pipeline in this segment was described as stronger than a year ago, giving "further confidence that this rebound appears sustainable."

  • On acquisitions, Hanna announced "we closed 2 tuck-in acquisitions: one, a modular company in the Midwest and the other a portable storage company in the Southeast." He described these as providing "additional fleet, team members and customer relationships which help accelerate our pace of growth."

  • On outlook, Hanna commented, "we are upwardly adjusting our outlook" due to improving economic indicators and customer activity, and described the company's "strategic focus on the modular business and expanding the value of our modular solutions capabilities." He emphasized ongoing geographic expansion and the hiring of new sales representatives.

  • CFO Keith E. Pratt reported, "Total revenues increased 11% to $235.6 million, and adjusted EBITDA increased 3% to $86.5 million." Pratt noted Mobile Modular's total revenues increased 8% to $156 million and all operational revenue streams grew. He highlighted a decrease in adjusted EBITDA for the segment due to higher inventory center and SG&A expenses linked to strategic hiring. Pratt also reported TRS-RenTelco's adjusted EBITDA increased 7% to $19.3 million, and Portable Storage's adjusted EBITDA was $9.8 million, up 15% sequentially but down year-over-year.

Outlook

  • Pratt stated, "we have upwardly revised our full year financial outlook, and we currently expect total revenue between $925 million and $960 million, adjusted EBITDA between $347 million and $356 million and gross rental equipment capital expenditures between $115 million and $125 million." For the remainder of 2025, "we expect adjusted EBITDA to be at a similar level in the third and fourth quarters."

Financial Results

  • Total revenues for Q2 were $235.6 million and adjusted EBITDA was $86.5 million. Mobile Modular total revenues reached $156 million, with rental revenues up 5%, rental-related services up 11%, and sales revenues up 13%. Portable Storage reported total revenue of $23.3 million and rental revenues of $16.9 million, with rental margins at 83%. TRS-RenTelco's total revenues were $36.4 million, with rental revenues increasing by 7% and rental margins improving to 44%. At quarter end, net borrowings were $573 million and the ratio of funded debt to last 12 months adjusted EBITDA was 1.6:1.

Q&A

  • Scott Andrew Schneeberger, Oppenheimer: Asked about EBITDA balance for Q3 and Q4, to which Pratt responded, "we expect will be broadly similar in each of those two quarters." On visibility for Mobile Modular, Pratt said it's "a bit fluid" due to the current demand environment. Schneeberger also inquired about education rentals, and Hanna replied, "I think our education year this year is going to be -- that's going to be good. We realized orders a little bit later in the year ... but we're happy with the volume of orders we got."

  • Schneeberger questioned the modest increase to adjusted EBITDA guidance, and Pratt referred to "a lot of moving parts," emphasizing that the sales business can fluctuate. He explained, "If things go well, we might do better than we're currently expecting and sort of factoring into our outlook."

  • Unidentified Analyst, CJS Securities: Asked about the pricing gap in modular rentals, Pratt responded, "the gap is slightly different. I think it was 41% when we looked at the same statistics a quarter ago, but order of magnitude still similar."

  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research: Asked about geographic focus of sales hires, Hanna explained, "what we like to do in some of those locations and in some new locations that are adjacent, we want to have additional sales power." On Modular Plus growth, Hanna said, "we anticipate being able to keep up that pace."

  • Marc Frye Riddick, Sidoti: Inquired about technology investments, Hanna highlighted the need to "have the latest technology in terms of our IT systems." On the M&A pipeline, Hanna stated, "It's something that we're paying close attention to. ... We've got a nice pipeline of opportunities, a lot of conversations going on."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a constructive but cautious tone, probing on the sustainability of growth, visibility into guidance, and the impact of strategic expenses. Schneeberger described the EBITDA outperformance as "meaningful" but pressed on why the guidance raise was "less meaningful."

  • Management maintained a cautiously optimistic tone, with Hanna stating "we are cautiously optimistic that economic conditions will improve" and Pratt emphasizing a "realistic view" around guidance. Management described positive momentum in several areas but acknowledged "fluid" conditions and did not overstate the outlook. Both prepared remarks and Q&A responses reflected measured confidence.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment has shifted slightly more positive, with more upwardly adjusted guidance and acknowledgment of improved trends, in contrast to prior caution around macro uncertainty and tariffs.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company raised its full-year revenue and EBITDA outlook for 2025 compared to the previous quarter, reflecting improved market conditions and stronger customer activity. Strategic focus on modular solutions and geographic expansion remains consistent, but there is increased emphasis on sales hiring and M&A.

  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on timing and sustainability of growth, while in the previous quarter, questions centered on macro headwinds and tariff risks.

  • Management’s tone this quarter was more confident, with explicit upward revisions to guidance, compared to the more cautious stance and slight guidance trim in Q1.

  • Key metrics such as total revenues, adjusted EBITDA, and segment growth showed improvement over Q1, with sequential improvements in Portable Storage and sustained momentum in TRS-RenTelco.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing uncertainty in the demand environment, noting "with the current demand environment, there can be delays, there can be uncertainty."

  • Portable Storage continues to face challenges from "lower commercial construction project activity," although sequential trends are improving.

  • Elevated SG&A and inventory center expenses linked to strategic hiring and fleet preparation are being absorbed this year to support long-term growth.

  • Analysts inquired about the sustainability of recent improvements and the degree of visibility into year-end results, reflecting a measured approach to assessing risk.

Final Takeaway

McGrath RentCorp leadership emphasized that the company is entering the second half of 2025 with cautious optimism, citing improved revenue and EBITDA outlooks, continued strategic investment in modular solutions, successful tuck-in acquisitions, and expanding sales capacity. Management believes that these actions position the company to capitalize on gradual improvements in the demand environment and deliver value to shareholders while remaining vigilant about ongoing market uncertainties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Origin Bancorp raises margin guidance to 3.70% for Q4 2025 through balance sheet optimization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Origin Bancorp, Inc. (OBK) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Drake Mills stated that "we introduced Optimize Origin, our plan to deliver sustainably leap level financial performance," with a near-term goal of achieving a 1% ROA run rate by Q4 2025 and a longer-term target for ROA to reach the top quartile of peers. Mills highlighted "efficiencies within our branch network, improved the overall profitability of our commercial banking team, restructured our mortgage business and taken multiple actions to optimize our balance sheet," resulting in approximately $34 million in annual earnings improvement on a pretax pre-provision basis. Mills also noted, "On July 1, we took an additional step towards our goal of high-level profitability by increasing our ownership of Argent Financial to 20%, which triggers the equity method of accounting. Next year, we anticipate this will drive additional income of approximately $6 million."
  • President and CEO of Origin Bank, Martin Hall, reported that since the end of Q2 '24, Origin reduced FTE headcount by 8% across the bank and 18% in commercial banking teams, emphasizing data-driven decisions. He also noted, "Our team achieved strong C&I production in Q2, where on an average basis, our C&I loans grew at an annualized rate of nearly 13%." Hall detailed ongoing efficiency and revenue enhancement projects, estimating $4 million to $5 million in annualized pretax earning benefits from these initiatives.
  • Chief Risk Officer Jim Crotwell stated, "During the second quarter, we achieved approximately $50 million in additional desired reductions, bringing the total targeted reductions to approximately $250 million since we began this initiative." He shared that total past due loans held for investment decreased to 0.88% at quarter end compared to 0.96% for Q1 2025, while net charge-offs for the quarter were $2.3 million, a reduction from Q1.
  • CFO William Wallace stated, "In Q2, we reported diluted earnings per share of $0.47." He highlighted, "Net interest margin expanded 17 basis points during the quarter to 3.61%." Wallace added, "Given the loan and deposit declines on a year-to-date basis, we have reduced 2025 growth guidance to low single digits for both."

Outlook

  • Wallace announced, "we increased our margin guidance by 20 basis points to 3.70% in 4Q '25 and by 10 basis points to 3.55% for the full year, plus or minus 5 basis points." He explained that modeling now considers "25 basis point Fed funds rate cuts in September and December." Mills indicated continued focus on ROA growth and discipline in pricing and client selection.
  • Deposit costs are expected to remain in line with historical beta trends, and the noninterest-bearing deposit ratio is anticipated to stay in the 22% to 23% range through 2025. Noninterest income guidance, excluding notable items, was raised to low double-digit growth for Q4 '25 over Q4 '24, primarily due to the new accounting treatment for Argent.

Financial Results

  • Diluted earnings per share for Q2 2025 was reported as $0.47. The quarter included a net expense of $15.6 million from notable items, equivalent to $0.39 in EPS pressure. Loans increased 1.3% sequentially but decreased 1.0% when excluding mortgage warehouse. Total deposits declined 2.6% during the quarter and 2.3% excluding brokered deposits. Net interest margin was 3.61%. Noninterest income, excluding notable items, increased to $16 million from $15.5 million in Q1. Tangible book value grew to $33.33, and the TCE ratio rose to 10.9%.
  • The company repurchased 136,399 shares at an average price of $31.84 and authorized a new $50 million repurchase plan through July 2028.

Q&A

  • Matt Olney, Stephens: Asked about the ramp in net interest margin to 3.70% in Q4 and key drivers. Wallace responded that tailwinds from loan repricing and securities repricing are expected to continue, and the guidance includes two modeled Fed cuts. He noted, "you could see margin coming in kind of towards the lower end of that guidance range...But if not, then we could come in towards the higher end."
  • Olney asked about loan growth, recent paydowns, and timing for normalized growth. Hall noted that "clients that expected rates to be decreased by now...put some projects on hold" and some used excess deposits to pay for projects rather than debt. Hall projects mid-single-digit annualized commercial growth for the back half of 2025 and mid- to high single digits for 2026, but emphasized focus on ROA and disciplined growth.
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about capital utilization, share buybacks, and M&A appetite. Mills indicated comfort with redeeming $75 million of sub debt in the fourth quarter and said, "we love M&A," particularly lift-out strategies, but would consider core deposit opportunities.
  • Rose asked about staying under $10 billion in assets. Mills replied, "the model shows that we'll be right at that $10 billion at year-end with expected growth."
  • Wood Lay, KBW: Asked about the timing of securities restructuring. Wallace explained the trade had been planned in Q1 but was executed in Q2 due to market volatility and improved spreads, stating, "As far as large loss trades go, this is it."
  • Lay inquired about potential for increased Argent ownership. Hall replied, "you're going to see us stay consistent kind of in that 20% to 25% ownership level."
  • Manuel Navas, D.A. Davidson: Sought details on Argent's growth and potential write-up. Wallace confirmed, "the valuation that those transactions occurred and will result in a write-up...of about $7 million" in Q3, with a $6 million annualized income benefit expected starting in 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to cautiously positive tone, pressing for details on margin expansion, loan growth recovery, capital deployment, and expense opportunities, but without overt skepticism or negativity.
  • Management tone was confident and focused, repeatedly emphasizing disciplined execution and optimism about achieving profitability and efficiency targets. Mills expressed, "I have full confidence that our employees will continue to deliver exceptional value to all our stakeholders."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted to increased confidence about reaching ROA and margin goals ahead of schedule, while analysts remained focused on understanding the outlook for growth and expense management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for loan and deposit growth was reduced to low single digits from a previous expectation of mid to high-single-digit growth. Margin guidance was increased to 3.70% for Q4 2025, compared to 3.50% previously. Cost controls and headcount reductions were more pronounced in Q2. Analysts continued to focus on margin, loan growth, capital utilization, and progress on Optimize Origin, mirroring prior quarter priorities but with greater emphasis on sustainability of improvements and timing of growth recovery. Management's confidence in achieving profitability targets increased compared to Q1, with more explicit projections and acknowledgment of economic headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged "economic uncertainty exists" and flagged slowed loan growth due to tariffs and interest rate expectations. There is pressure from deposit and loan declines, with some customers using excess cash to pay down loans. Credit metrics showed stable performance with slight increases in nonperforming loans. Management continues to monitor the impact of market competition on loan and deposit pricing and remains vigilant about maintaining disciplined growth.

Final Takeaway

Origin Bancorp management emphasized the successful execution of efficiency, profitability, and balance sheet optimization initiatives, raising net interest margin guidance and maintaining strong capital flexibility. While economic headwinds persist, the company is confident in its ability to deliver improved returns ahead of schedule, driven by disciplined client selection, strategic investments, and continued focus on ROA growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pembina Pipeline reaches settlement with shippers on Alliance Pipeline
data.description:
Pembina Pipeline (NYSE:PBA) +1.2% post-market Thursday after announcing a negotiated settlement with shippers on the Canadian portion of the Alliance Pipeline and filed for regulatory approval with the Canada Energy Regulator.

The 10-year settlement, effective November 1, includes revised term-differentiated tolls that are expected to reduce existing long-term firm tolls by an average of 14% on a volume weighted average basis, the company said.

The agreement, which follows a November 2024 order from the CER to submit a detailed toll application, was supported by a Shipper Committee consisting of more than 30 members in a vote on July 23.

Pembina (NYSE:PBA) said it expects the financial impact to Alliance from the settlement over the next 10 years will be a ~$50M/year reduction in long-term firm service revenue, plus the impact of the new revenue sharing provision.

Separately, Alliance said it is soliciting non-binding expressions of interest for a prospective regional short-haul expansion providing transportation capacity to Alberta's industrial heartland; as part of the proposed offering, Alliance has initiated a feasibility study for up to 350M cf/day of incremental capacity with an anticipated in-service date in late 2029.

data.title: Deckers signals $1.38B–$1.42B Q2 revenue target amid robust HOKA and UGG growth, while navigating tariff headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Stefano Caroti stated fiscal year 2026 began with HOKA and UGG both outperforming expectations, highlighting "revenue growing 17% versus last year to $965 million and diluted earnings per share increasing 24% to $0.93." He emphasized "HOKA delivered its largest quarter in its history," with significant international momentum and strong sell-through during key model transitions.
  • Caroti reported "HOKA performance came in ahead of our expectations," with global wholesale up 30% and DTC increasing 3% globally, citing EMEA and APAC regions as key growth drivers. U.S. performance was "aligned with our expectations." He described ongoing expansion into international markets and selective retail store growth, particularly in China and Europe.
  • The CEO outlined new execution initiatives for HOKA, including "adjusting product life cycles," "tightening marketplace inventory targets on outgoing models," and enhancing the DTC loyalty program. He noted "the consumer is showing a strong affinity for the updates to the HOKA brand's three largest franchises."
  • On UGG, Caroti stated global revenue increased 19% to $265 million, led by a 30% jump in wholesale and strong international growth, particularly in EMEA and China. The success of UGG’s PeakMod and 365 initiatives in sandal and sneaker segments was highlighted.
  • CFO Steven J. Fasching said, "Deckers again delivered another quarter of strong results that came in above our expectations for the first quarter." He detailed "Total company revenue was $965 million, up 17% versus the prior year," and explained gross margin was 55.8%, down from 56.9% last year due to "unfavorable channel mix with wholesale growing faster than DTC, increased promotion across UGG and HOKA... and higher freight rates."

Outlook

  • The company is not providing a formal full-year outlook for fiscal year 2026, citing "continued macroeconomic uncertainty related to the global trade policy and difficulty predicting impact on the consumer environment and purchasing behavior."
  • Fasching reiterated, "our fiscal year 2026 framework includes the following: from a revenue perspective, we expect HOKA to continue as our fastest-growing brand, UGG continuing to grow, international to outpace U.S. growth and wholesale to outpace DTC in the near term."
  • For Q2 fiscal 2026, Deckers expects revenue in the range of $1.38 billion to $1.42 billion, with "HOKA increasing approximately 10%... and UGG increasing at least mid-single digits." Gross margin is expected to be between 53.5% and 54%, with diluted EPS in the range of $1.50 to $1.55 compared to last year's $1.59.
  • Fasching noted, "we would expect to face a total of $185 million of unmitigated impact to our cost of goods sold in fiscal year 2026, up from our previously provided estimate of up to $150 million," due to increased tariffs.

Financial Results

  • Deckers reported $965 million in revenue for Q1 2026, a 17% increase year-over-year. HOKA contributed $653 million and UGG $265 million, with international revenue up 50%.
  • Gross margin for the quarter was 55.8%, down 110 basis points from last year, attributed to "unfavorable channel mix," increased promotional activity, and higher freight, partially offset by favorable product mix and currency effects.
  • SG&A for the quarter was $373 million, 38.6% of revenue, with growth driven by increased marketing, logistics, and rent costs due to store expansion. Tax rate increased to 24% from 22.5% last year.
  • Inventory was $849 million (up 13% year-over-year), and Deckers ended June 2025 with $1.7 billion in cash and equivalents, with no outstanding borrowings. The company repurchased $183 million in shares during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Jay Daniel Sole, UBS: Asked about HOKA’s Q2 guidance and the wholesale vs. DTC channel performance, as well as inventory status and product pipeline. Fasching explained the "10% growth" reflects some earlier wholesale shipments and expects "a little bit more balanced growth between the two channels." Caroti indicated the market is "largely clean of Bondi 8s and Clifton 9s... Arahi 8... is performing very well."
  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas: Questioned DTC growth, store expansion, and the impact of tariffs on order books. Fasching said improvements are expected in U.S. DTC performance and noted "the framework still holds" for mid-teens HOKA growth and mid-single digit UGG growth, pending tariff clarity.
  • Adrienne Yih-Tennant, Barclays: Inquired about the price increase strategy and gross margin outlook. Caroti said price increases are "selective and staggered." Fasching added that more margin pressure is expected in the back half of fiscal 2026 due to tariffs.
  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: Sought further detail on HOKA innovation and cash usage for buybacks. Caroti highlighted investments in "innovation, in design, in color and engineering," with upcoming launches. Fasching stated strong cash supports share repurchases.
  • Rick Patel, Raymond James: Asked about HOKA's international growth drivers and SG&A outlook. Caroti pointed out "revenue growth is outpacing door expansion and sell-through continues to outpace sell-in." Fasching emphasized continued "well-managed" investments in global brand building.
  • Samuel Poser, Williams Trading: Probed retail vs. e-commerce performance and promotional activity. Fasching said retail stores "performed better than e-commerce significantly." Caroti confirmed "no material decline in performance for the products that we have raised price on."
  • John David Kernan, TD Cowen: Asked about U.S. vs. international growth and HOKA DTC inflection. Fasching expects "incremental improvement in Q2" for both regions and noted DTC is "encouraged" but expects gradual gains rather than dramatic shifts.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but focused questioning on tariff impacts, DTC performance, and gross margin headwinds. The tone was pressing, especially on topics of inventory, pricing strategy, and channel mix.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone, frequently reiterating brand strength and proactive initiatives. Phrases like "we're confident," "we believe," and "we continue to be excited" were used, with some defensiveness in discussing gross margin and promotional activity.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management sustained confidence, but analysts showed increased concern about tariffs and margin pressures. Management's prepared remarks were more upbeat, while their Q&A responses were more cautious and detailed.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained cautious, with no formal full-year outlook due to "continued macroeconomic uncertainty," in line with last quarter's approach.
  • Strategic focus remains on international expansion, innovation investment, and balancing wholesale and DTC channels, consistent with prior quarter themes.
  • Analysts' questions shifted more heavily toward tariff impact, price execution, and DTC recovery, reflecting growing concerns over margin compression and consumer response to higher prices.
  • Key metrics such as revenue growth, international sales, and inventory levels all increased sequentially, but gross margin declined from the previous year.
  • Management’s confidence in their brands and operating model remained strong, but their commentary on macro risks and mitigation strategies became more granular.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted "macroeconomic uncertainty related to the global trade policy," specifically noting tariff-related cost headwinds now estimated at $185 million for fiscal 2026.
  • Gross margin pressure is expected to persist, driven by "increased tariffs, higher levels of promotion, upgraded materials on key styles and higher ocean freight rates."
  • Analysts raised concerns about DTC performance, inventory management, and the effectiveness of price increases and promotional strategies.
  • Management is implementing mitigation strategies, including selective price increases, tighter expense control, and continued evaluation of cost-sharing with suppliers.

Final Takeaway

Deckers Brands opened fiscal year 2026 with robust revenue and earnings growth, driven by record HOKA performance and strong international momentum for both HOKA and UGG. While facing heightened uncertainty from increased tariffs and macroeconomic pressures, management emphasized disciplined execution, strategic investments, and brand innovation as core strengths. The company remains confident in its ability to achieve long-term growth, supported by a solid balance sheet, proactive cost management, and continued consumer demand for its flagship brands.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cadence Bank outlines 11%-15% loan growth and 10%-12% revenue growth targets for 2025 following recent acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cadence Bank (CADE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO James Rollins highlighted an active quarter for Cadence, stating, "On the M&A front, we announced our acquisition of Industry Bancshares on April 25. We then completed our acquisition of First Chatham Bank effective May 1, and we closed the industry transaction on July 1.... We are excited about the opportunity to expand our presence in Georgia and Central Texas." He also noted, "Adjusted net income from continuing operations increased to $137.5 million or $0.73 per share, and adjusted ROA was 1.14% for the quarter." Rollins emphasized organic loan growth of $1.1 billion for the quarter, describing it as "12.6% annualized," and cited strong pipelines across business lines. He added, "Our core customer deposit balances also showed growth, which offset some intentional runoff in brokered and a seasonal decline in public fund balances."
  • CFO Valerie Toalson stated, "Our pretax pre-provision net revenue for the second quarter increased to an all-time high of $206 million, up over 8% from the prior quarter... Average loans were up a little over $800 million in the quarter, while period-end loans grew by $1.4 billion, a $1.1 billion in organic growth and close to $400 million from the First Chatham acquisition." Toalson also explained, "Net interest revenue increased $15 million or 4% as a result of the robust loan growth as well as added securities... The NIM declined 6 basis points in the second quarter to 3.40%." She highlighted, "Loan yields were 6.34% in the quarter, up 1 basis point from the first quarter, and new and renewed loans in the quarter came on the books at just over 7%."

Outlook

  • Cadence Bank updated guidance to reflect both the First Chatham and Industry acquisitions. Toalson reported, "We continue to expect solid loan demand for the latter half of the year, bringing full year loan growth, including the acquisitions to between 11% and 15%. This, combined with full year core customer deposit growth of between 12% and 15%, supports our expectation for total revenue growth between 10% and 12%." She further stated, "We forecast continued operating leverage with expenses increasing between 7% and 9% in support of the growth in the balance sheet and continued investment in our future. Combined with stable credit, we expect these results will continue to drive strong EPS performance for us throughout the rest of this year."

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net income from continuing operations reached $137.5 million or $0.73 per share. Pretax pre-provision net revenue was $206 million, described as an "all-time high." Total adjusted revenue was $476 million, an increase of $28 million or 6%. Net interest revenue was up $15 million or 4% from the prior quarter. Organic loan growth was $1.1 billion, with period-end loans up by $1.4 billion including acquisitions. Organic core customer deposits increased at a 4.4% annualized rate. Noninterest-bearing deposits as a percentage of total deposits increased to 22.6%. Net charge-offs for the quarter were $21 million or 24 basis points annualized. Tangible book value increased to $22.94 per share, and CET1 capital stood at 12.2%.
  • Toalson noted, "Adjusted noninterest revenue reflected great performance really across the board, increasing $13 million or 15% compared to the first quarter."

Q&A

  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research: Asked about net interest margin (NIM) rebound potential and further restructuring. Rollins answered, "On the industry piece, we've done what we wanted to do... But right now, that's not on the top burn." Toalson added, "We expect some continued improvement there. So we're actually very optimistic about the net interest margin and anticipate that, that would increase as we go through the rest of the year."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about updated revenue guidance and purchase accounting assumptions. Rollins replied, "With the increased guide on loan growth, that's going to produce organic revenue growth that you're seeing in the increased guide there." Toalson said, "Our interest rate or the securities... we assumed a 2.5% liquidity mark... we're able to dispose of those securities at less than half of that. So we're coming in much better on that front."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Queried about new origination volumes versus paydown activity and changes in accretion. Fisackerly explained, "In the first quarter, we saw a lot in the merchant real estate portfolio from a paydown standpoint and from our midstream energy. We saw that kind of slow, while at the same time, continuing really good origination." Toalson clarified, "The accretion would be a little bit less. But of course, your upfront impact to your tangible book value is less."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about core deposit growth and CD renewal rates. Toalson responded, "We had about $3.8 billion in originations of CDs that were at just shy of 360... there's an opportunity to continue to see a little bit of compression in that overall amount."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth sustainability, margin rebound, and integration of acquisitions, with a generally positive tone but pressing for clarity on guidance details and acquisition impacts.
  • Management maintained a confident outlook, repeatedly emphasizing strong pipelines, execution of integration, and positive expectations for NIM and revenue, as evidenced by Toalson's statement, "We are actually very optimistic about the net interest margin and anticipate that, that would increase as we go through the rest of the year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive regarding growth prospects, while analysts shifted from cautious optimism to more direct inquiries about execution and forward guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was updated to higher full-year growth ranges for loans and deposits (now 11%-15% and 12%-15% respectively, compared to steady guidance previously).
  • Strategic focus shifted toward integration and immediate contribution of recent acquisitions, with senior management highlighting rapid M&A execution.
  • Key metrics improved quarter-over-quarter, including net income, revenue, and loan growth, whereas in Q1, management had highlighted stable but less pronounced growth.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter concentrated more on the sustainability of improved performance and integration impacts, compared to the prior quarter’s focus on macroeconomic uncertainty and margin stability.
  • Management exuded greater confidence in achieving targets, frequently referencing robust pipelines and successful acquisition integrations, while in Q1, the tone was more cautious given broader economic uncertainties.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged increased legal costs due to "final resolution of a legal matter."
  • Slight rise in criticized and classified loans linked to "a handful of credits," but balances remain within historical ranges.
  • Credit trends described as stable, with net charge-offs and nonperforming asset levels consistent with expectations.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about deposit cost pressures, integration risks, and the durability of strong loan pipelines, to which management responded by underscoring balance sheet strength and ongoing monitoring.

Final Takeaway

Cadence Bank’s management underscored a quarter marked by record loan growth, robust earnings, and rapid integration of two acquisitions, while raising full-year targets for loan, deposit, and revenue growth. Management expressed optimism regarding margin improvement and continued operating leverage, and emphasized that successful integration combined with strong pipelines positions the company for continued EPS performance through 2025 and into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
